reasoning: The market estimate of 51% likely overreacted to the sharp drop in the Atlanta Fed's Q1 GDPNow forecast (which hit -2.8% in early March). The negative print is driven primarily by a massive jump in imports as businesses front-run Trump tariffs. While this could result in a negative Q1 advance estimate, national accounting mathematics mean this tariff anxiety will act as a baseline effect that strongly pushes Q2 GDP positive (as imports revert to normal levels or fall). Because the resolution defines a recession strictly as two consecutive quarters of negative GDP, and the resolution date is Dec 26, 2025 (meaning only Q1+Q2 or Q2+Q3 are viable windows, since Q4 advance won't be out), Q2 needs to be negative. A rebounding Q2 makes a technical recession incredibly difficult to achieve inside 2025. Although Goldman Sachs downgraded general US growth due to tariffs, they still project it to be strongly positive at +1.7% for the year, largely removing the threat of consecutive true contractions. Thus, I estimate the true probability significantly lower than the crowd, although the chaos around DOGE and tariffs warrants maintaining some risk premium above the historical base rate.
